302	\begin{lemma}[L4 anchor — Noether normalisation over a field]
303	  \label{lem:noether_normalization_fg}
304	  \lean{exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg}
305	  \mathlibok
306	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
307	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(R\) a non-trivial finite-type \(k\)-algebra. Then there
308	  exist \(s \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective \(k\)-algebra homomorphism
309	  \(k[X_1, \dots, X_s] \to R\) that is module-finite, i.e.\ \(R\) is a finite module
310	  over a polynomial subalgebra on algebraically independent generators.
311	\end{lemma}
312	
313	\begin{lemma}
314	
315	
316	\leanok
317	[L4a — clearing one equation's denominators]
318	  \label{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_clear_one_denominator}
320	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
321	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1755-L1759).
322	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
323	  % "If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for
324	  %  coefficients of equations of integral dependence
325	  %  satisfied by a finite set of
326	  %  algebra generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$,
327	  %  we see that $B_g$ is finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
328	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (common denominator).}
329	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), and let
330	  \(p \in K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) be a polynomial with coefficients in \(K\) (for instance,
331	  one coefficient polynomial of an equation of integral dependence over
332	  \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\)). Then there exists \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), such that \(p\)
333	  lies in the image of the coefficient-extension map
334	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\); equivalently, \(p\) has all of its
335	  coefficients in \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\).
336	\end{lemma}
337	
338	\begin{proof}
339	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1755-L1759).
340	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (common denominator).}
341	  The polynomial \(p\) has finitely many non-zero coefficients
342	  \(c_1, \dots, c_r \in K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Each \(c_j\) is a fraction
343	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) with \(a_j \in A\) and \(s_j \in A \setminus \{0\}\). Put
344	  \(g := s_1 \cdots s_r \in A\); since \(A\) is a domain and each \(s_j \ne 0\), also
345	  \(g \ne 0\). For each \(j\) the denominator \(s_j\) divides \(g\), so
346	  \(c_j = a_j / s_j\) lies in the subring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\). Hence every
347	  coefficient of \(p\) lies in \(A_g\), and \(p\) is the coefficient-extension of a
348	  polynomial in \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
349	\end{proof}
350	
351	\begin{lemma}\leanok
352	[L4 — Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators]
353	  \label{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}
354	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_localizationAway_finite_mvPolynomial}
355	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator,
356	        lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
357	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, normalisation step
358	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1747-L1758).
359	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
360	  % "By Noether normalisation lemma, there exist elements $b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B$,
361	  %  such that $K\otimes_AB$ is finite over its subalgebra $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$ and
362	  %  the elements $b_1,\ldots, b_n$ are algebraically independent over $K$. [...]
363	  %  If $g\ne 0$ in $A$ is chosen to be a `common denominator' for coefficients of
364	  %  equations of integral dependence satisfied by a finite set of algebra
365	  %  generators for $K\otimes_AB$ over $K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$, we see that $B_g$ is
366	  %  finite over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
367	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (Noether
368	  normalisation with denominators).}
369	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K\) and \(B\) a finite-type
370	  \(A\)-algebra that is a domain with \(B_K = K \otimes_A B \ne 0\). Write
371	  \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\) and \(B_g = B[g^{-1}]\) for the localisations away from \(g\)
372	  and its image. Then there exist a natural number \(n\), an element \(g \in A\) with
373	  \(g \ne 0\), and an injective \(A_g\)-algebra homomorphism
374	  \[
375	    \varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \longrightarrow B_g,
376	  \]
377	  where \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is the polynomial ring on \(n\) algebraically
378	  independent variables, such that \(B_g\) is module-finite over the image polynomial
379	  subalgebra via \(\varphi\). The injectivity of \(\varphi\) records the algebraic
380	  independence of the generators \(b_j := \varphi(X_j)\), and module-finiteness is
381	  taken over the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\).
382	\end{lemma}
383	
384	\begin{proof}
385	  \uses{lem:noether_normalization_fg, lem:gf_clear_one_denominator}
386	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1747-L1758).
387	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (Noether normalisation with denominators).}
388	  \emph{Step 1 — Noether normalisation over \(K\).} Apply Noether normalisation over the
389	  field \(K\) (\cref{lem:noether_normalization_fg}) to the finite-type \(K\)-algebra
390	  \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\): there are algebraically independent
391	  \(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n \in B_K\) with \(B_K\) module-finite over
392	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). Clearing denominators, each \(\bar b_j\) is
393	  represented by some \(b_j \in B\) (writing \(1 \otimes b\) as \(b\)).
394	
395	  \emph{Step 2 — clear the integral-dependence denominators to a single \(g\).} Fix a
396	  finite set \(\sigma\) of algebra generators of \(B_K\) over
397	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). By Step 1 each generator's image
398	  \(1 \otimes x \in B_K\) is integral over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). For each
399	  \(x \in \sigma\), a standard integrality-and-localization descent argument yields a
400	  non-zero \(a_x \in A\) such that \(a_x \cdot (1 \otimes x)\)
401	  is integral over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Multiplying over the finite
402	  set, set \(g_1 := \prod_{x \in \sigma} a_x\) and \(g := g_0 \cdot g_1\) (with \(g_0\) the
403	  Step-1 denominator witness). After inverting \(g\), every chosen generator becomes
404	  integral over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) and the generators generate \(B_g\) as an algebra;
405	  hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). (The single-coefficient
406	  lemma \cref{lem:gf_clear_one_denominator} provides the same denominator-clearing primitive
407	  at the level of one coefficient polynomial; the proof discharges a whole generator
408	  at once using this integrality-descent step, folding that primitive over the coefficients
409	  internally.)
410	
411	  \emph{Step 3 — AlgHom assembly.} The algebra homomorphism \(\varphi\) of the statement
412	  is the \(A_g\)-algebra map \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\) sending \(X_j \mapsto b_j\),
413	  where \(b_j \in B_g\) is the image of \(b_j \in B\). We establish injectivity and
414	  module-finiteness through a comparison with the situation over \(K\).
415	
416	  \emph{Step 3a — the comparison map \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\).} Since \(g \ne 0\) in the
417	  domain \(A\) and \(A \subseteq K\), the image of \(g\) is a unit of the field \(K\), so
418	  it is invertible in \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\). Hence the canonical \(A\)-algebra map
419	  \(B \to B_K\), \(b \mapsto 1 \otimes b\), sends \(g\) to a unit and, by the universal
420	  property of localisation, factors through a canonical \(A\)-algebra map
421	  \[
422	    \nu : B_g = B[g^{-1}] \longrightarrow B_K, \qquad \nu(b/g^k) = (1 \otimes b)\,(1 \otimes g)^{-k}.
423	  \]
424	  The map \(\nu\) is injective. Indeed, \(B\) is a domain, and both \(B_g\) and \(B_K\) are
425	  localisations of \(B\) at multiplicative subsets of the non-zerodivisors: \(B_g\) inverts
426	  the powers of \(g\), while \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\) inverts (in particular) the images of
427	  all non-zero elements of \(A\), a multiplicative set containing the powers of \(g\). A
428	  localisation of a domain at a set of non-zerodivisors is injective into any further such
429	  localisation. Concretely, the natural map \(B \to B_K\) is injective on the domain \(B\)
430	  (its kernel is the set of \(b\) annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\), which in a
431	  domain over which \(A\) acts faithfully is \(0\)), so \(\nu(b/g^k) = 0\) forces
432	  \(1 \otimes b = 0\), hence \(b = 0\) and \(b/g^k = 0\). Under \(\nu\) the lifts
433	  \(b_j \in B_g\) of the Noether generators map to the elements \(\bar b_j \in B_K\) of
434	  Step 1: \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\).
435	
436	  \emph{Step 3b — injectivity of \(\varphi\) by algebraic-independence descent
437	  \(K \to A_g\).} Injectivity of the \(A_g\)-algebra map
438	  \(\varphi : A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \to B_g\), \(X_j \mapsto b_j\), is equivalent to the
439	  algebraic independence of the family \((b_j)\) over \(A_g\): a non-zero element of the
440	  kernel is exactly a non-trivial \(A_g\)-polynomial relation \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\).
441	  The localisation \(A_g = A[g^{-1}] \subseteq K\) embeds in the fraction field, giving an
442	  injection of \(A_g\)-algebras \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\). Now suppose
443	  \(P \in A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) is non-zero with \(P(b_1, \dots, b_n) = 0\) in \(B_g\).
444	  Applying the injective map \(\nu\) and using \(\nu(b_j) = \bar b_j\) together with the fact
445	  that \(\nu\) is an \(A\)-algebra map extending scalars to \(K\), we obtain
446	  \(P^K(\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n) = 0\) in \(B_K\), where \(P^K\) is the image of \(P\)
447	  under the coefficient inclusion \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow K[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
448	  Since \(A_g \hookrightarrow K\) is injective, \(P \ne 0\) gives \(P^K \ne 0\); but the
449	  \(\bar b_j\) are algebraically independent over \(K\) (Step 1), so the only \(K\)-polynomial
450	  vanishing on them is \(0\), a contradiction. Hence no non-trivial relation exists, \((b_j)\)
451	  is algebraically independent over \(A_g\), and \(\varphi\) is injective.
452	
453	  \emph{Step 3c — module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\).} By Step 1,
454	  \(B_K\) is module-finite over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\); fix a finite set of algebra
455	  generators of \(B_K\) over \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\) (Step 2). Each such generator
456	  satisfies a monic equation of integral dependence whose coefficients are polynomials in
457	  \(K[\bar b_1, \dots, \bar b_n]\). The element \(g\) of Step 2 was chosen as a common
458	  denominator for the (finitely many) coefficient polynomials of these equations, so that
459	  after inverting \(g\) every such coefficient already lies in
460	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n] = \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Each chosen generator is the
461	  \(\nu\)-image of an element of \(B_g\), and its integral-dependence equation is the
462	  \(\nu\)-image of the corresponding monic equation holding in \(B_g\) with coefficients in
463	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\); since \(\nu\) is injective, that equation holds in \(B_g\)
464	  itself. Thus each generator of \(B_g\) is integral over the subalgebra
465	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\), and these generators generate \(B_g\) as an
466	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi\)-algebra. A finite-type algebra that is integral over a
467	  subalgebra is module-finite over it; hence \(B_g\) is module-finite over
468	  \(\operatorname{im}\varphi = A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Transported along the injection
469	  \(\varphi\), this is exactly module-finiteness of \(B_g\) over \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) for
470	  the algebra structure that \(\varphi\) puts on \(B_g\), as claimed. This recovers the
471	  source's conclusion that \(B_g\) is finite over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\).
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
475	[injectivity of a localisation lift]
476	  \label{lem:gf_isLocalization_lift_injective}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalization_lift_injective}
478	  Let \(S\) be a localisation of a ring \(R\) at a multiplicative set \(M\), and let
479	  \(g : R \to P\) be a ring homomorphism sending every element of \(M\) to a unit of
480	  \(P\), so that it induces the lift \(\widehat g : S \to P\). If both the localisation
481	  map \(R \to S\) and \(g\) itself are injective, then \(\widehat g\) is injective.
482	\end{lemma}
483	\begin{proof}
484	  The lift is characterised by \(\widehat g(r/m) = g(r)\,g(m)^{-1}\). By the universal
485	  criterion for injectivity of a localisation lift, \(\widehat g\) is injective iff for
486	  all \(r_1, r_2 \in R\) the equality of localisation images
487	  \((r_1 \mapsto S) = (r_2 \mapsto S)\) implies \(g(r_1) = g(r_2)\). Both the hypothesis
488	  and the conclusion of that implication reduce, via injectivity of \(R \to S\) and of
489	  \(g\), to the single condition \(r_1 = r_2\); hence the implication holds and
490	  \(\widehat g\) is injective. (This helper is applied twice in
491	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators}, to the comparison maps \(\nu : B_g \to B_K\)
492	  and \(\psi : A_g \to K\).)
493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\begin{lemma}
496	
497	
498	\leanok
499	[L5a — the generic-rank short exact sequence]
500	  \label{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_generic_rank_ses}
502	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
503	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1765).
504	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
505	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the
506	  %  domain $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact
507	  %  sequence of $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
508	  %  $$0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$$
509	  %  where $T$ is a finite torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$."
510	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (generic-rank SES).}
511	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(N\) a finite module over the
512	  polynomial ring \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure
513	  through the scalar tower \(A \to P_d \to N\)). Then there exist \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and
514	  an injective \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) whose cokernel
515	  \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\) is a torsion \(P_d\)-module. Equivalently, there is
516	  a short exact sequence of \(P_d\)-modules
517	  \[
518	    0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
519	    \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
520	  \]
521	
522	  The integer \(m\) is the \emph{generic rank} of \(N\): the dimension of the
523	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\)-vector space
524	  \(\mathrm{Frac}(P_d) \otimes_{P_d} N\), i.e.\ of the localisation of \(N\) at the
525	  non-zero-divisors of the domain \(P_d\). This step lives over \(P_d\) directly —
526	  \emph{no inversion of any element \(g \in A\) is required here}; the element \(g\) of
527	  Nitsure's argument enters only at the reindexing step \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{proof}
531	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1765).
532	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (generic-rank SES).}
533	  Write \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\); since \(A\) is a noetherian domain, so is \(P_d\)
534	  (Hilbert basis theorem, and a polynomial ring over a domain is a domain). Let
535	  \(Q = \mathrm{Frac}(P_d)\) be its fraction field and
536	  \(N_Q := Q \otimes_{P_d} N\) the localisation of \(N\) at the non-zero-divisors of
537	  \(P_d\); as \(N\) is finite over \(P_d\), \(N_Q\) is a finite-dimensional
538	  \(Q\)-vector space. Put \(m := \dim_Q N_Q\), the generic rank.
539	
540	  Choose a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\) and clear denominators: each basis vector is a
541	  \(Q\)-unit multiple of the image of some \(v_i \in N\), so the images of
542	  \(v_1, \dots, v_m \in N\) again form a \(Q\)-basis of \(N_Q\). In particular the family
543	  \((v_i)_{i}\) is linearly independent over \(Q\); restricting scalars along the
544	  injection \(P_d \hookrightarrow Q\), it is linearly independent over \(P_d\). Let
545	  \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) be the \(P_d\)-linear map
546	  \((c_1, \dots, c_m) \mapsto \sum_i c_i v_i\). Linear independence of \((v_i)\) over
547	  \(P_d\) makes \(\varphi\) injective.
548	
549	  Set \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\). Because the images of \(v_1, \dots, v_m\)
550	  already span \(N_Q\) over \(Q\), the localised map \(\varphi \otimes Q : Q^{\oplus m}
551	  \to N_Q\) is an isomorphism, so \(Q \otimes_{P_d} T = 0\). A finite \(P_d\)-module whose
552	  localisation at the non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\) vanishes is torsion; hence \(T\) is a
553	  torsion \(P_d\)-module. This is the asserted short exact sequence.
554	\end{proof}
555	
556	\begin{lemma}[L5b anchor — a finite torsion module has a non-zero-divisor annihilator]
557	  \label{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
558	  \lean{Submodule.annihilator_top_inter_nonZeroDivisors}
559	  \mathlibok
560	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
561	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727)
562	  % -- the same torsion-annihilation primitive, here provided in general by Mathlib.
563	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
564	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
565	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
566	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
567	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
568	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) a finite torsion \(R\)-module. Then the
569	  annihilator of \(M\) meets the non-zero-divisors of \(R\): the set
570	  \((\top : \operatorname{Submodule} R\,M).\mathrm{annihilator} \cap R^{\circ}\) is
571	  non-empty, where \(R^{\circ}\) denotes the non-zero-divisors of \(R\).
572	\end{lemma}
573	
574	\begin{lemma}\leanok
575	[L5b.1 — annihilator extraction for the torsion module]
576	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_annihilator}
578	  \uses{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors}
579	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, base case $n=-1$
580	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1724-L1727).
581	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
582	  % "each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite
583	  %  generating set, and a common multiple of corresponding annihilating
584	  %  elements, we see there exists an $f\ne 0$ in $A$ with $fM=0$."
585	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (torsion annihilation).}
586	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 0\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
587	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\). Then there exists a non-zero
588	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\); consequently the \(P_d\)-action on \(T\) factors
589	  through \(P_d/(F)\) and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
590	\end{lemma}
591	
592	\begin{proof}
593	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1724-L1727).
594	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (torsion annihilation).}
595	  The ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is a noetherian domain (a polynomial ring over the
596	  noetherian domain \(A\)), and \(T\) is a finite torsion \(P_d\)-module. By
597	  \cref{lem:annihilator_meets_nonZeroDivisors} the annihilator of \(T\) meets the
598	  non-zero-divisors of \(P_d\); choose \(F\) in that intersection. In the domain \(P_d\) a
599	  non-zero-divisor is exactly a non-zero element, so \(F \ne 0\), and \(F \cdot T = 0\).
600	  Since \(F\) annihilates \(T\), the \(P_d\)-module structure descends along the quotient
601	  map \(P_d \twoheadrightarrow P_d/(F)\); the image of any finite \(P_d\)-generating set of
602	  \(T\) generates \(T\) over \(P_d/(F)\), so \(T\) is finite over \(P_d/(F)\).
603	\end{proof}

605	\subsubsection{Nagata change-of-variables machinery}
606	\label{sec:gf_nagata_machinery}
607	
608	The Nagata normalisation lemma \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} is assembled from
609	technical helper lemmas implementing the triangular change of variables and the
610	radix-encoding degree bookkeeping, following the Nagata trick of
611	\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 over a noetherian domain. Throughout, \(k\) is a domain,
612	\(n \ge 0\), and \(f \in k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is a fixed polynomial; we write
613	\(\mathrm{up} := 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}}(f)\) and the radix weights \(r_i := \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\)
614	for \(i = 0, \dots, n\) (so every exponent occurring in \(f\) is a base-\(\mathrm{up}\) digit).
615	The distinguished variable is \(X_0\).
616	
617	\begin{definition}\leanok
618	[Nagata substitution \(T_1\)]
619	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T1}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T1}
621	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
622	  For a scalar \(c \in k\), the substitution \(T_1(c)\) is the \(k\)-algebra endomorphism
623	  of \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) determined on generators by
624	  \[
625	    X_0 \mapsto X_0, \qquad X_i \mapsto X_i + c\,X_0^{\,r_i} \quad (i \ne 0).
626	  \]
627	  It is triangular in the variables \(X_i\) (\(i \ne 0\)) over the distinguished variable
628	  \(X_0\); this is the device realising the support-dimension drop.
629	\end{definition}
630	
631	\begin{lemma}\leanok
632	[\(T_1\) is invertible by the opposite shift]
633	  \label{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
634	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.t1_comp_t1_neg}
635	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
636	  For every \(c \in k\), \(T_1(c) \circ T_1(-c) = \mathrm{id}\); the Nagata substitution is
637	  invertible, with inverse the substitution of opposite coefficient.
638	\end{lemma}
639	\begin{proof}
640	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1}
641	  It suffices to check the two composites agree on each generator: \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), and
642	  \(X_i \mapsto X_i + (-c)X_0^{r_i} + c\,X_0^{r_i} = X_i\) for \(i \ne 0\).
643	\end{proof}
644	
645	\begin{definition}\leanok
646	[Nagata automorphism \(T\)]
647	  \label{def:gf_nagata_T}
648	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T}
649	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T1, lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}
650	  \textit{Source: Nitsure \S4 (Nagata change of variables).}
651	  The Nagata change of variables is the \(k\)-algebra automorphism \(T := T_1(1)\) of
652	  \(k[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) — i.e.\ \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{\,r_i}\) (\(i \ne 0\)) and
653	  \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\) — packaged as an algebra equivalence with inverse \(T_1(-1)\) via
654	  \cref{lem:gf_t1_comp_t1_neg}.
655	\end{definition}
656	
657	\begin{lemma}\leanok
658	[Exponent bound is preserved by listing]
659	  \label{lem:gf_lt_up}
660	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.lt_up}
661	  If every entry of an exponent vector \(v\) satisfies \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\), then
662	  every entry of the list of values of \(v\) is also \(< \mathrm{up}\).
663	\end{lemma}
664	\begin{proof}
665	  The list of values of \(v\) consists exactly of the entries \(v_i\); each satisfies
666	  \(v_i < \mathrm{up}\) by hypothesis.
667	\end{proof}
668	
669	\begin{lemma}\leanok
670	[Injectivity of the radix encoding]
671	  \label{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
672	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.sum_r_mul_ne}
673	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
674	  Let \(v, w\) be exponent vectors with all entries \(< \mathrm{up}\) and \(v \ne w\). Then
675	  the weighted base-\(\mathrm{up}\) sums differ:
676	  \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\), where \(r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}\).
677	\end{lemma}
678	\begin{proof}
679	  \uses{lem:gf_lt_up}
680	  The map \(v \mapsto \sum_i \mathrm{up}^{\,i} v_i\) is the value of the base-\(\mathrm{up}\)
681	  digit string \(v\); since every digit is \(< \mathrm{up}\) (\cref{lem:gf_lt_up}), this
682	  encoding is injective by uniqueness of base-\(\mathrm{up}\) expansions, so distinct
683	  \(v \ne w\) give distinct sums.
684	\end{proof}
685	
686	\begin{lemma}\leanok
687	[\(X_0\)-degree of a transformed monomial]
688	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t}
689	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_zero_t}
690	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
691	  For a non-zero scalar \(a \ne 0\) and exponent vector \(v\), the degree in \(X_0\) of the
692	  transformed monomial is the radix value
693	  \[
694	    \deg_{X_0}\!\big(T(a\,X^v)\big) = \sum_i r_i v_i, \qquad r_i = \mathrm{up}^{\,i}.
695	  \]
696	\end{lemma}
697	\begin{proof}
698	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T}
699	  Under \(T\), \(a\,X^v \mapsto a\,X_0^{v_0}\prod_{i \ne 0}(X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\). Read as
700	  a polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), each factor
701	  \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) has \(X_0\)-degree \(r_i v_i\) and leading coefficient \(1\);
702	  the degrees add to \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) (the hypothesis \(a \ne 0\) keeps the top term from
703	  vanishing over the domain \(k\)).
704	\end{proof}
705	
706	\begin{lemma}\leanok
707	[Distinct monomials acquire distinct \(X_0\)-degrees]
708	  \label{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
709	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
710	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
711	  For distinct support exponents \(v \ne w\) of \(f\), the transformed monomials
712	  \(T(c_v X^v)\) and \(T(c_w X^w)\) (where \(c_v, c_w\) are the corresponding coefficients
713	  of \(f\)) have distinct degrees in \(X_0\), making the top \(X_0\)-degree of \(T(f)\) unique.
714	\end{lemma}
715	\begin{proof}
716	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t, lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne}
717	  By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_zero_t} the two degrees are \(\sum_i r_i v_i\) and
718	  \(\sum_i r_i w_i\). Each support exponent is bounded by the total degree of \(f\), hence
719	  \(< \mathrm{up} = 2 + \deg_{\mathrm{tot}} f\); radix-injectivity
720	  \cref{lem:gf_sum_r_mul_ne} then yields \(\sum_i r_i v_i \ne \sum_i r_i w_i\).
721	\end{proof}
722	
723	\begin{lemma}\leanok
724	[Leading coefficient of a transformed monomial]
725	  \label{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
726	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
727	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
728	  For any exponent \(v\) with coefficient \(c_v := [X^v]\,f\), viewing \(T(c_v X^v)\) as a
729	  univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over \(k[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), its leading coefficient in
730	  \(X_0\) is the constant \(c_v \in k\).
731	\end{lemma}
732	\begin{proof}
733	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, def:gf_nagata_T1}
734	  Each factor \((X_i + X_0^{r_i})^{v_i}\) is monic in \(X_0\) with leading coefficient
735	  \(1\); a product of monics is monic, so the leading coefficient of \(T(c_v X^v)\) in
736	  \(X_0\) is exactly the scalar \(c_v\).
737	\end{proof}
738	
739	\begin{lemma}\leanok
740	[Leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) is a non-zero constant]
741	  \label{lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq}
742	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.T_leadingcoeff_eq}
743	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
744	  Suppose \(f \ne 0\). Then there is a support exponent \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\) such that
745	  the leading coefficient of \(T(f)\) in \(X_0\) equals the constant \(c_v := [X^v]\,f \ne 0\).
746	  Thus \(T(f)\) is, up to the non-zero scalar \(c_v\), monic in \(X_0\).
747	\end{lemma}
748	\begin{proof}
749	  \uses{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne, lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}
750	  Expand \(f = \sum_{w \in \mathrm{supp}\,f} c_w X^w\). By \cref{lem:gf_degreeOf_t_ne_of_ne}
751	  the transformed monomials \(T(c_w X^w)\) have pairwise distinct \(X_0\)-degrees, so one of
752	  them — say at \(v\) — strictly dominates the others; the leading term of
753	  \(T(f) = \sum_w T(c_w X^w)\) is then that of \(T(c_v X^v)\), whose leading coefficient is
754	  the constant \(c_v\) by \cref{lem:gf_leadingCoeff_finSuccEquiv_t}. Since
755	  \(v \in \mathrm{supp}\,f\), \(c_v \ne 0\).
756	\end{proof}
757	
758	\begin{lemma}\leanok
759	[Naturality of the variable-splitting isomorphism under base change]
760	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
761	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
762	  For any ring homomorphism \(\varphi : A \to B\) and polynomial \(q \in A[X_0, \dots, X_m]\),
763	  the isomorphism \(A[X_0, \dots, X_m] \cong (A[X_1, \dots, X_m])[X_0]\) is natural with
764	  respect to applying \(\varphi\) coefficientwise.
765	\end{lemma}
766	\begin{proof}
767	  By induction on \(q\): the isomorphism is additive and multiplicative, and agrees with
768	  \(\varphi\) on constants and variables, so it commutes with \(\varphi\) on all polynomials.
769	\end{proof}
770	
771	\begin{lemma}\leanok
772	[Constant inclusion under the variable-splitting isomorphism]
773	  \label{lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
774	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
775	  For \(s \in R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), the composite of the inclusion
776	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n] \hookrightarrow R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) (sending \(X_j \mapsto X_j\))
777	  with the isomorphism \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) sends \(s\)
778	  to the constant polynomial \(s \in (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\).
779	\end{lemma}
780	\begin{proof}
781	  By induction on \(s\): a constant and each variable \(X_j\) (\(j \ge 1\)) map to themselves,
782	  and the isomorphism is an algebra map, so it sends products and sums of such to constants in
783	  the outer polynomial ring.
784	\end{proof}
785	
786	\begin{lemma}\leanok
787	[L5b.2 — Nagata change of variables: monic in the distinguished variable]
788	  \label{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
789	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}
790	  \uses{def:gf_nagata_T, lem:gf_T_leadingcoeff_eq, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_map_comm}
791	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
792	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
793	  % the Nagata substitution is the standard device realising the support-dimension drop.
794	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
795	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
796	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
797	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
798	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
799	  Let \(A\) be a domain, \(n \ge 0\), and \(0 \ne F \in A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\). Then there
800	  exist a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) and a
801	  non-zero \(g \in A\) such that, after inverting \(g\), the image of \(e(F)\) in
802	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\), viewed as a univariate polynomial in \(X_0\) over
803	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_n]\), has a \emph{unit} leading coefficient. Equivalently, \(e(F)\)
804	  is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\) over \(A_g\).
805	\end{lemma}
806	
807	\begin{proof}
808	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
809	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop, Nagata normalisation).}
810	  Apply the Nagata substitution \(X_i \mapsto X_i + X_0^{r^{\,i}}\) for \(i = 1, \dots, n\)
811	  and \(X_0 \mapsto X_0\), with \(r\) larger than every exponent occurring in \(F\): it is a
812	  triangular (hence invertible) \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(A[X_0, \dots, X_n]\).
813	  Under this substitution the distinct monomials of \(F\) acquire distinct degrees in
814	  \(X_0\), so \(e(F)\), expanded as a polynomial in \(X_0\), has a unique top-degree term
815	  whose coefficient is the coefficient \(c \in A\) of the top total-degree monomial of
816	  \(F\); since \(F \ne 0\) this \(c \ne 0\). Put \(g := c\). The degree of \(e(F)\) in
817	  \(X_0\) equals the total degree of \(F\), and its leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is the
818	  constant \(g\). Passing to \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\), the image of \(g\) is a unit, so the
819	  image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to the unit \(g\).
820	\end{proof}
821	
822	\begin{lemma}[L5b engine anchor — quotient by a monic univariate polynomial is finite]
823	  \label{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite}
824	  \lean{Polynomial.Monic.finite_quotient}
825	  \mathlibok
826	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
827	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(f \in S[X]\) a monic polynomial. Then the quotient
828	  \(S[X]/(f)\) is a module-finite \(S\)-module (indeed free of rank \(\deg f\)).
829	\end{lemma}
830	
831	\begin{lemma}\leanok
832	[L5b.3 — single-variable elimination engine (shared)]
833	  \label{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic}
834	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_of_monic_lastVar}
835	  \uses{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite, lem:gf_finSuccEquiv_rename_succ}
836	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
837	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768);
838	  % the division algorithm in one variable is the standard mechanism of the support drop.
839	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
840	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
841	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
842	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
843	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (single-variable elimination).}
844	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), and \(p \in R[X_0, \dots, X_n]\) a
845	  polynomial whose image under the isomorphism
846	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \cong (R[X_1, \dots, X_n])[X_0]\) has a \emph{unit} leading
847	  coefficient — i.e.\ \(p\) is monic in \(X_0\) up to a unit. Then the
848	  quotient \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
849	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) (embedded by \(X_j \mapsto X_j\), \(j = 1, \dots, n\)).
850	\end{lemma}
851	
852	\begin{proof}
853	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
854	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (single-variable elimination).}
855	  Write \(S := R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\) and let
856	  \(\Phi : R[X_0, \dots, X_n] \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } S[X_0]\) be the standard \(R\)-algebra
857	  isomorphism, which sends \(X_0\) to the univariate variable and embeds \(S\) as the constants. Put \(q := \Phi(p) \in S[X_0]\); by hypothesis its leading
858	  coefficient \(u\) is a unit, so the associate \(\tilde q := u^{-1} \cdot q\) is monic and
859	  \((\tilde q) = (q)\) in \(S[X_0]\). The isomorphism \(\Phi\) carries the ideal \((p)\) onto
860	  \((q) = (\tilde q)\), hence descends to a ring isomorphism
861	  \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p) \cong S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) that is \(S\)-linear, because \(S\) sits
862	  inside both sides as the constants (respectively as the coefficient subring
863	  \(R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\)). By \cref{lem:polynomial_monic_quotient_finite} the quotient
864	  \(S[X_0]/(\tilde q)\) is module-finite over \(S\); transporting finiteness along the
865	  \(S\)-linear isomorphism shows \(R[X_0, \dots, X_n]/(p)\) is module-finite over
866	  \(S = R[X_1, \dots, X_n]\).
867	\end{proof}
868	
869	\begin{lemma}
870	
871	
872	\leanok
873	[L5b — torsion reindex onto fewer variables]
874	  \label{lem:gf_torsion_reindex}
875	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.gf_torsion_reindex}
876	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
877	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
878	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
879	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
880	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
881	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
882	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
883	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
884	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
885	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}

890	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
891	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
892	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
893	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
894	\end{lemma}
895	
896	\begin{proof}
897	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
898	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}

1	\chapter{The tautological quotient and the universal property of $\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)$}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianQuot}
3	
4	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
5	
6	The previous chapter constructed the Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
7	\(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing the affine charts \(U^I\)
8	(\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) along the transition isomorphisms
9	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
10	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
12	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
13	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
14	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
15	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
16	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
17	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
18	of the trivial sheaf.
19	
20	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
21	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
22	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
23	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
24	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
25	
26	\section{Infrastructure: locally free sheaves and module gluing}
27	\label{sec:grquot_infra}
28	
29	The constructions of this chapter -- the universal quotient sheaf
30	\(\mathcal{U}\), the tautological quotient \(u\), and the functor of points -- rest
31	on two pieces of foundational machinery for sheaves of modules. The first is the
32	predicate ``locally free of rank \(d\)'' (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}),
33	which records that a sheaf of modules is, on the members of some open cover,
34	isomorphic to the trivial bundle \(\mathcal{O}^{d}\); it is the condition cut out
35	in the definition of the Grassmannian functor. That predicate is the project-local
36	\(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}\) already set up in the Quot-scheme
37	chapter (\cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}), which this chapter reuses verbatim;
38	the only foundational machinery genuinely new to this chapter is the second piece:
39	a gluing primitive that descends a sheaf of modules (and a morphism of such
40	sheaves) from per-chart data along an open cover, given a transition cocycle
41	compatible with the cover's own gluing; it is the vehicle that assembles the
42	per-chart free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}^{d}_{U^I}\) and the per-chart quotients
43	\(u^I\) into the global \(\mathcal{U}\) and \(u\), and is set up below.
44	
45	\begin{definition}
46	
47	\leanok
48	[Gluing a sheaf of modules along an open cover]
49	  \label{def:scheme_modules_glue}
50	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.glue}
51	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data}
52	  Let \(D\) be a glue datum of schemes (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with index set \(J\), charts \(U_i\),
53	  pairwise overlaps \(V_{ij}\), open immersions
54	  \(f_{ij} : V_{ij} \hookrightarrow U_i\), and transition isomorphisms
55	  \(t_{ij} : V_{ij} \xrightarrow{\sim} V_{ji}\) satisfying the glue datum's
56	  cocycle conditions; write \(X\) for the glued scheme and
57	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\) for the canonical open immersions of the
58	  charts. Suppose given:
59	  \begin{itemize}
60	    \item for each \(i \in J\), a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}_i\) on the chart \(U_i\);
61	    \item for each pair \((i,j)\), a \emph{transition isomorphism} of sheaves of
62	      modules on the overlap \(V_{ij}\),
63	      \[
64	        g_{ij} \;:\; f_{ij}^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_i
65	          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
66	        (t_{ij} \circ f_{ji})^{*}\,\mathcal{M}_j,
67	      \]
68	      comparing the two charts' sheaves after restriction (pullback) to the
69	      common overlap \(V_{ij}\);
70	  \end{itemize}
71	  \emph{subject to the following module cocycle conditions}, compatible with the
72	  scheme-level cocycle of \(D\) (its triple transition data \(t_{ij}\) and the
73	  overlap immersions \(f_{ij}\)), which are required as hypotheses on the family
74	  \((g_{ij})\):
75	  \begin{enumerate}
76	    \item[(C1)] \emph{self-identity:} the self-transition is the identity,
77	      \(g_{ii} = \mathrm{id}\) on \(f_{ii}^{*}\mathcal{M}_i\) (with
78	      \(V_{ii}\) and \(t_{ii}\) the diagonal data of \(D\));
79	    \item[(C2)] \emph{triple-overlap multiplicativity:} over each triple overlap
80	      \(V_{ij} \cap V_{ik}\) one has
81	      \(g_{jk} \circ g_{ij} = g_{ik}\), the two sides being compared after
82	      transport through the scheme-level triple transition of \(D\)
83	      (\(t_{ij}\) and the overlap immersions \(f_{ij}, f_{jk}, f_{ik}\)) so that
84	      all three pullbacks land on the same sheaf -- the multiplicative,
85	      \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued cocycle condition.
86	  \end{enumerate}
87	  Conditions (C1) and (C2) are exactly the descent datum for the Zariski cover
88	  \(\{\iota_i\}\); it is subject to them that the gluing below is well-defined and
89	  the glued sheaf is characterised up to unique isomorphism. Out of this data the
90	  construction produces:
91	  \begin{enumerate}
92	    \item a glued sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{M}\) on the glued scheme \(X\); and
93	    \item for each \(i \in J\) a \emph{restriction isomorphism}
94	      \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\,\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{M}_i\)
95	      identifying the pullback of \(\mathcal{M}\) along \(\iota_i\) with the
96	      \(i\)-th chart sheaf, the \(\rho_i\) being compatible with the transition
97	      isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\) over the overlaps.
98	  \end{enumerate}
99	  The pair \((\mathcal{M}, (\rho_i)_i)\) is characterised up to unique
100	  isomorphism by the restriction property: \(\mathcal{M}\) is the unique sheaf of
101	  modules on \(X\) (up to canonical isomorphism) restricting to each
102	  \(\mathcal{M}_i\) compatibly with \((g_{ij})\). The same primitive descends
103	  morphisms: given two such glued data \((\mathcal{M}_i, g_{ij})\) and
104	  \((\mathcal{N}_i, h_{ij})\) with glued sheaves \(\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{N}\), and
105	  a family of \(\mathcal{O}_{U_i}\)-linear morphisms
106	  \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) that commute with the transition
107	  isomorphisms on every overlap, there is a unique morphism
108	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) with
109	  \(\rho_i^{\mathcal{N}} \circ \iota_i^{*}\phi = \phi_i \circ \rho_i^{\mathcal{M}}\)
110	  for every \(i\) -- the descent of \(\mathrm{Hom}\) along the cover.
111	
112	  \emph{Construction.} A sheaf of modules on \(X\) is equivalent to a compatible
113	  family of sheaves on the members of an open cover of \(X\) together with
114	  identifications on the overlaps -- effective descent for the Zariski cover
115	  \(\{\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\}\). The charts \(U_i\), being the images of
116	  the glue datum's objects, form such a cover, with intersections
117	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) identified with the overlaps \(V_{ij}\) via the structural data
118	  of \(D\). Pulling the sections of an abstract \(\mathcal{M}\) back to each chart
119	  must reproduce \(\mathcal{M}_i\); the transition isomorphisms \(g_{ij}\),
120	  satisfying the cocycle conditions, are exactly the descent datum that makes the
121	  family \((\mathcal{M}_i)\) glue, so the descent produces \(\mathcal{M}\) with
122	  the asserted restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i\). The morphism statement is the
123	  descent of morphisms for the same cover: a morphism on \(X\) is a family of
124	  morphisms on the charts agreeing on overlaps, and the compatibility of the
125	  \(\phi_i\) with \((g_{ij}, h_{ij})\) is precisely the agreement condition,
126	  giving the unique glued \(\phi\). This specialises the general descent of sheaves
127	  of modules to the concrete open cover supplied by a scheme glue datum,
128	  and is the form in which the Grassmannian's tautological bundle and quotient are
129	  built.
130	
131	  \emph{Realisation (construction path).} Concretely, instantiate \(D\) as the
132	  Grassmannian glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}), with glued scheme
133	  \(X = \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) and its canonical open cover by the charts
134	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\). The glued sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is built from
135	  the per-chart sheaves \(\mathcal{M}_i\) and the overlap transition isomorphisms
136	  \(g_{ij}\) satisfying the module cocycle conditions, and is pinned down by the
137	  restriction isomorphisms \(\rho_i : \iota_i^{*}\mathcal{M} \xrightarrow{\sim}
138	  \mathcal{M}_i\) compatible with the \(g_{ij}\). For an open immersion
139	  \(\iota_i : U_i \hookrightarrow X\), the pullback \(\iota_i^{*}\) restricts sheaves
140	  of modules on \(X\) to sheaves of modules on \(U_i\). The transition isomorphisms
141	  \(g_{ij}\), forming a cocycle for this cover, constitute a descent datum; gluing the
142	  local sections yields \(\mathcal{M}\) together with the restriction isomorphisms
143	  \(\rho_i\), where both the assembly and the characterisation up to unique isomorphism
144	  rest on the locality of sections of a sheaf -- a compatible family of sections over
145	  the cover, agreeing on the overlaps, extends to a unique global section.
146	
147	  The morphism-descent half is the same locality argument applied to homomorphisms:
148	  a family \(\phi_i : \mathcal{M}_i \to \mathcal{N}_i\) commuting with the transition
149	  isomorphisms over every overlap is a compatible family of local morphisms, and the
150	  uniqueness of gluing of sections promotes it to a unique global
151	  \(\phi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of modules on \(X\) restricting
152	  to each \(\phi_i\) through the \(\rho_i\).
153	\end{definition}
154	
155	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
156	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
157	
158	\emph{Infrastructure: scalar endomorphisms.} Realising a matrix of regular
159	functions as a morphism of free sheaves of modules has no off-the-shelf primitive
160	in Mathlib; it is assembled from two small project-local helpers that turn a global
161	function into a scalar endomorphism of the structure sheaf, after which the matrix
162	is reconstituted entry-by-entry over a finite biproduct.
163	
164	\begin{definition}[Global section of the unit module]
165	  \label{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.globalUnitSection}
167	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
168	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) a global section of
169	  the structure sheaf. Viewing \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as the unit module
170	  \(\mathbf{1} \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}), the
171	  section \(a\) determines a global section of \(\mathbf{1}\): on each open
172	  \(V \subseteq X\) take the restriction \(a|_V \in \Gamma(V, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the
173	  family \((a|_V)_V\) being compatible with the restriction maps because
174	  restriction is functorial. This is the section of the unit sheaf attached to a
175	  global function.
176	\end{definition}
177	
178	\begin{definition}[Scalar endomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)]
179	  \label{def:gr_scalarEnd}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scalarEnd}
181	  \uses{def:gr_globalUnitSection, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
182	  For \(a \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the \emph{scalar endomorphism}
183	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(a) : \mathcal{O}_X \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is the
184	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear endomorphism of the unit module given by multiplication
185	  by \(a\). It is obtained from the global section of \cref{def:gr_globalUnitSection}
186	  under the canonical identification of endomorphisms of the unit module
187	  \(\mathbf{1}\) with its global sections, \(\End(\mathbf{1}) \cong
188	  \Gamma(X, \mathbf{1})\); concretely, on each open \(V\) it acts as
189	  multiplication by \(a|_V\). These scalar endomorphisms are the matrix-entry
190	  building blocks of the chart quotient.
191	\end{definition}
192	
193	\begin{definition}
194	
195	\leanok
196	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
197	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
199	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_scalarEnd}
200	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
201	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
202	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
203	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
204	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
205	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
206	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
207	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
208	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
209	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
210	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
211	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
212	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
213	  \[
214	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
215	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
216	  \]
217	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
218	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
219	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
220	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
221	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
222	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
223	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
224	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
225	
226	  \emph{Realisation.} The free sheaves \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) and
227	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) are the finite biproducts (coproducts) of copies of
228	  the unit module \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_{U^I}\), indexed by \(\{1,\dots,r\}\)
229	  and \(\{1,\dots,d\}\). Each entry \(X^I_{p,q} \in R^I\) of the universal matrix
230	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) is a global function on \(U^I = \Spec R^I\),
231	  obtained from \(R^I\) through the canonical identification
232	  \(\Gamma(\Spec R^I, \mathcal{O}) \cong R^I\); the corresponding scalar
233	  endomorphism \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(X^I_{p,q}) : \mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\)
234	  (\cref{def:gr_scalarEnd}) is the \((p,q)\)-component of \(u^I\). Assembling these
235	  components over the index biproducts -- the universal property of the biproduct
236	  packaging a matrix of component morphisms into a single morphism of the free
237	  sheaves -- yields \(u^I\). This entry-by-entry assembly via the finite biproduct
238	  is the substitute for a (non-existent) Mathlib ``matrix \(\mapsto\) morphism of
239	  free sheaves'' primitive.
240	\end{definition}
241	
242	\begin{lemma}[The chart quotient is an epimorphism]
243	  \label{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}
244	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap_epi}
245	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
246	  The chart quotient \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
247	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules.
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient}
252	  We exhibit a section, so that \(u^I\) is a \emph{split} epimorphism and hence an
253	  epimorphism. Let \(\iota_I : \{1,\dots,d\} \hookrightarrow \{1,\dots,r\}\) be the
254	  inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, \(k \mapsto \iota_I(k)\), the
255	  \(k\)-th element of \(I\) in increasing order. It induces a morphism of free
256	  sheaves \(s_I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\), the
257	  coordinate inclusion sending the \(k\)-th basis section to the
258	  \(\iota_I(k)\)-th. We claim
259	  \[
260	    u^I \circ s_I \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}}.
261	  \]
262	  Both sides are morphisms out of the free rank-\(d\) sheaf, which is the coproduct
263	  of \(d\) copies of the unit module; by the universal property of that coproduct
264	  it suffices to check the equality on each coordinate, i.e.\ to compare component
265	  matrices. The \((p,k)\)-component of \(u^I \circ s_I\) is the entry
266	  \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)}\) of the universal matrix at the \(I\)-indexed column, which
267	  is precisely the \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\). Since that minor is the
268	  \(d \times d\) identity (the columns of \(X^I\) indexed by \(I\), read through the
269	  order isomorphism \(\{1,\dots,d\} \cong I\), form the identity block --
270	  \cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}), one has \(X^I_{p,\iota_I(k)} = \delta_{p,k}\),
271	  so \(u^I \circ s_I\) has the identity component matrix and equals
272	  \(\mathrm{id}\). Hence \(s_I\) splits \(u^I\) and \(u^I\) is a split
273	  epimorphism, in particular an epimorphism.
274	\end{proof}
275	
276	\begin{definition}
277	
278	\leanok
279	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
280	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
281	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
282	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
283	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
284	    def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
285	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
286	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
287	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
288	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
289	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
290	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
291	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
292	  %  by putting
293	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
294	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
295	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
296	  \[
297	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
298	  \]
299	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
300	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
301	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
302	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
303	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
304	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
306	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
307	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
308	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
309	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
310	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
311	\end{definition}
312	
313	\begin{definition}
314	
315	\leanok
316	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
317	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
319	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
320	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle,
321	    def:scheme_modules_glue}
322	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
323	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
324	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
325	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
326	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
327	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
328	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
329	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
330	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
331	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
332	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
333	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
334	  \[
335	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
336	  \]
337	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
338	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
339	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
340	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
341	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
342	  \[
343	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
344	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
345	  \]
346	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
347	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
348	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
349	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
350	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
351	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
352	\end{definition}
353	
354	\section{The functor of points}
355	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
356	
357	\begin{definition}
358	
359	\leanok
360	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
361	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
363	  \uses{def:is_locally_free_of_rank, def:quot_functor}
364	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
365	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
366	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
368	  % "represents the
369	  %  contravariant functor
370	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
371	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
372	  %  set of all equivalence classes
373	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
374	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
375	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
376	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
377	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
378	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
379	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
380	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
381	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
382	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
383	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
384	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
385	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
386	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
387	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
388	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
389	  \[
390	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
391	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
392	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
393	  \]
394	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
395	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
396	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
397	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
398	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
399	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
400	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
401	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
402	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
403	\end{definition}
404	
405	\section{The universal property}
406	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
407	
408	\begin{theorem}
409	
410	\leanok
411	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
412	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
413	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
414	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
415	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
416	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
417	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
418	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
419	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
420	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
421	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
422	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
423	  %  contravariant functor
424	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
425	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
426	  %  set of all equivalence classes
427	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
428	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
429	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
430	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
431	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
432	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
433	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
434	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
435	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
436	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
437	  \[
438	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
439	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
440	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
441	    \qquad
442	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
443	      \;\longmapsto\;
444	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
445	  \]
446	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
447	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
448	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
449	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
450	  universal family.
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	\begin{proof}
454	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
455	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
456	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
457	    def:grassmannian_functor}
458	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
459	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
460	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
461	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
462	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
463	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
464	
465	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
466	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
467	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
468	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
469	  the composite
470	  \[
471	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
472	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
473	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
474	  \]
475	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
476	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
477	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
478	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
479	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
480	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
481	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
482	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
483	
484	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
485	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
486	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
487	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
488	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
489	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
490	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
491	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
492	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
493	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
494	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
495	
496	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
497	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
498	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
499	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
500	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
501	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
502	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
503	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
504	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
505	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
506	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
507	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
508	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
509	
510	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies

514	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
515	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
516	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
517	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.

520	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
521	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
522	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
523	\end{proof}

979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.

988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}

992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}

994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}

1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}

1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}
1210	
1211	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1212	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1213	
1214	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1215	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1216	
1217	\begin{lemma}
1218	
1219	
1220	
1221	\leanok
1222	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1223	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1225	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1226	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1227	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1228	\end{lemma}
1229	\begin{proof}
1230	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1231	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1232	\end{proof}
1233	
1234	\begin{lemma}
1235	
1236	
1237	
1238	\leanok
1239	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1240	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1242	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1243	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1244	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1245	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1246	  is free over \(A_f\).

1251	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1252	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1253	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}
1304	
1305	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1306	[Half 1 — G1 locality reduction over a finite basic-open cover]
1307	  \label{lem:gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1308	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_sections_of_basicOpen_finite_cover}
1309	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1310	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1311	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof, gluing step
1312	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2107-L2109).
1313	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1314	  % "Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$. Hence we conclude by
1315	  %  Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover}."
1316	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (gluing step,
1317	  Algebra Lemma \texttt{lemma-cover}).}
1318	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(X\), and
1319	  \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) is a finite
1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1323	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1328	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1340	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1341	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1342	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1343	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1344	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1345	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1346	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
