1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1359	
1360	\begin{lemma}[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1361	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1362	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1363	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1364	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1365	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1366	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1367	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1368	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1369	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1370	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1371	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1372	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1373	\end{lemma}
1374	
1375	\begin{lemma}[The transported family keeps its index type]
1376	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1377	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1378	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1379	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1380	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1381	  holds definitionally.
1382	\end{lemma}
1383	
1384	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness survives transport]
1385	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1386	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1387	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1388	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1389	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1390	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1391	\end{lemma}
1392	
1393	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1394	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1395	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1396	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1397	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1398	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1399	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1400	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1401	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1402	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1403	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1404	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1405	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1406	\end{lemma}
1407	
1408	\begin{proof}
1409	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1410	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1411	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1412	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1413	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1414	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1415	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1416	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1417	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1418	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1419	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1420	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1421	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1422	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1423	\end{proof}
1424	
1425	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1426	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1427	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1429	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1430	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1431	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1432	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1433	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1434	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1435	\end{lemma}
1436	
1437	\begin{proof}
1438	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1439	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1440	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1441	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1442	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1443	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1444	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1445	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1446	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1447	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1448	\end{proof}
1449	
1450	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1451	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1452	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1453	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1454	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1455	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1456	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1457	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1458	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1459	\end{lemma}
1460	
1461	\begin{proof}
1462	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1463	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1464	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1465	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1466	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1467	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1468	  finite generating family.
1469	\end{proof}
1470	
1471	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1472	[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1473	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1475	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1476	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1477	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1478	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1479	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1480	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1481	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1482	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1483	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1484	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1485	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1486	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1487	  cover of generated affines).}
1488	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a sheaf of modules of finite type,
1489	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1490	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1491	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1492	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1493	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1494	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1495	\end{lemma}
1496	
1497	\begin{proof}
1498	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1499	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1500	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1501	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1502	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1503	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1504	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1505	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1506	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1507	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1508	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1509	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1510	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1511	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1512	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1513	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1514	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1515	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1516	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1517	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1518	  affines.
1519	\end{proof}

1521	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1522	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1523	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}

1525	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1526	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1527	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1528	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1529	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1530	  this adjunction is the natural transformation

1536	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1537	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1538	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}

1542	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof

1544	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1545	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1546	  % epi over an affine.
1547	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1548	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1549	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1550	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1551	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1552	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1553	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1554	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1555	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1556	  \(B\)-module map.
1557	\end{lemma}
1558	
1559	\begin{proof}
1560	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1561	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1562	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1563	  affine).}
1564	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1565	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1566	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1567	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1568	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1569	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1570	
1571	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1572	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1573	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1574	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1575	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1576	  \[
1577	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1578	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1579	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1580	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1581	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1582	  \]
1583	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1584	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1585	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1586	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1587	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1588	
1589	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1590	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1591	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1592	\end{proof}
1593	
1594	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1595	[Half 2, seam 2$+$3 — a qcoh epimorphism with finite source sections has finite target sections]
1596	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1597	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated}
1598	  \uses{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi, lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}

1	\chapter{Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and graded sections}
2	\label{chap:Picard_SectionGradedRing}
3	
4	\providecommand{\PshMod}{\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}}
5	\providecommand{\ShMod}{\mathrm{SheafOfModules}}
6	\providecommand{\Modules}{\mathrm{Modules}}
7	
8	The Hilbert-polynomial route of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} represents the
9	degree-\(m\) graded component of a fibre by the global sections
10	\(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) of a twist of a coherent
11	sheaf by a tensor power of a line bundle, and it organises these components into
12	a graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
13	(\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and a graded module
14	\(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
15	\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
16	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}). Three pieces of that picture have no direct
17	Mathlib counterpart and must be built here, each in its own section:
18	
19	\begin{enumerate}
20	  \item \textbf{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
21	    (\cref{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}). Mathlib equips the category
22	    \(\PshMod\) of \emph{presheaves} of modules over a presheaf of commutative
23	    rings with a symmetric monoidal structure
24	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) and provides the
25	    sheafification functor \(\PshMod \to \ShMod\)
26	    (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); but the category
27	    \(\ShMod\) of \emph{sheaves} of modules carries \emph{no} monoidal
28	    structure. The tensor product of sheaves of modules
29	    \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) must therefore be built
30	    as the sheafification of the pointwise tensor presheaf, and the iterated
31	    tensor power \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) recursively from it.
32	  \item \textbf{Lax-monoidal global sections} (\cref{sec:sgr_lax_sections}).
33	    The global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is only \emph{lax} monoidal: from the
34	    pointwise-strict monoidality of the presheaf tensor product and the
35	    sheafification unit one obtains a natural multiplication
36	    \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(\mathcal{O}_X)} \Gamma(\mathcal{G})
37	    \to \Gamma(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\), together with
38	    the associativity and unit coherence those products must satisfy.
39	  \item \textbf{Graded-ring / graded-module assembly}
40	    (\cref{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}). The section multiplications and the
41	    tensor-power comparison isomorphisms assemble, through Mathlib's
42	    external-direct-sum graded API (\cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib},
43	    \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}), into the graded \emph{ring} structure
44	    on \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and the graded
45	    \emph{module} structure on
46	    \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
47	\end{enumerate}
48	
49	\section{Tensor powers of a sheaf of modules}
50	\label{sec:sgr_tensor_powers}
51	
52	We work over a fixed scheme \(X\) (in the application \(X = X_s\), a fibre over a
53	field \(\kappa(s)\)). Its category of sheaves of modules is
54	\(X.\Modules = \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\), with global-sections notation
55	\(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{M}) = \mathcal{M}(X)\) for the value of the underlying
56	presheaf on the top open. The structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is a sheaf of
57	commutative rings, so its underlying presheaf factors through the category of
58	commutative rings; this is the hypothesis under which Mathlib's presheaf-level
59	monoidal structure applies.
60	
61	\begin{lemma}[Monoidal structure on presheaves of modules]
62	  \label{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
63	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}
64	  \mathlibok
65	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
66	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal}).}
67	  Let \(R : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{CommRingCat}\) be a presheaf of
68	  commutative rings. Then the category \(\PshMod(R)\) of presheaves of modules
69	  over \(R\) carries a symmetric monoidal structure whose tensor product is
70	  computed objectwise: for presheaves of modules \(\mathcal{M}_1, \mathcal{M}_2\)
71	  and an object \(U\),
72	  \[
73	    (\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes \mathcal{M}_2)(U)
74	      \;=\; \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{R(U)} \mathcal{M}_2(U),
75	  \]
76	  with restriction maps the tensor products of the restriction maps. The unit is
77	  the presheaf \(R\) viewed as a module over itself, and the associator,
78	  unitors, and braiding are induced objectwise from those of
79	  \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\). In particular evaluation at a fixed object \(U\) is a
80	  \emph{strict} monoidal functor
81	  \(\PshMod(R) \to \mathrm{ModuleCat}(R(U))\).
82	\end{lemma}
83	
84	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification of presheaves of modules]
85	  \label{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
86	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}
87	  \mathlibok
88	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
89	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Sheafification}).}
90	  Let \(R\) be a sheaf of (commutative) rings on a site, with underlying
91	  presheaf \(R_0\). There is a sheafification functor
92	  \[
93	    (-)^{\#} \;:\; \PshMod(R_0) \longrightarrow \ShMod(R),
94	  \]
95	  left adjoint to the forgetful functor, together with the unit
96	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{M}_0} : \mathcal{M}_0 \to (\mathcal{M}_0)^{\#}\) of the
97	  adjunction. On underlying sheaves of abelian groups it agrees with the
98	  ordinary sheafification, and it sends a presheaf of \(R_0\)-modules to the
99	  associated sheaf of \(R\)-modules.
100	\end{lemma}
101	
102	\begin{lemma}[The structure sheaf as the unit module]
103	  \label{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
104	  \lean{SheafOfModules.unit}
105	  \mathlibok
106	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
107	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf}).}
108	  For a sheaf of rings \(R\) (in the application \(R = \mathcal{O}_X\)) the
109	  structure sheaf, viewed as a module over itself, is a distinguished object
110	  \(\mathbf{1} = \mathcal{O}_X \in \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the value at
111	  the zeroth tensor power, \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\), and its
112	  global sections \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) form the degree-zero component of
113	  the section graded ring.
114	\end{lemma}
115	
116	\begin{definition}[Presheaf-of-modules category in monoidal form]
117	  \label{def:monoidalPresheaf}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.MonoidalPresheaf}
119	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
120	  For a scheme \(X\), write \(\PshMod_{\otimes}(X)\) for the category of presheaves
121	  of modules over the underlying presheaf of (plain) rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\),
122	  obtained by composing the structure sheaf with the forgetful functor
123	  \(\mathrm{CommRingCat} \to \mathrm{RingCat}\). This is definitionally
124	  \(X.\PshMod\), but presented in the exact form for which Mathlib equips it with
125	  the symmetric monoidal structure of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib};
126	  it is the carrier on which the objectwise tensor product of
127	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} is taken.
128	\end{definition}
129	
130	\begin{definition}[Scheme-level module sheafification functor]
131	  \label{def:schemeModuleSheafification}
132	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafification}
133	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
134	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{module sheafification functor}
135	  \((-)^{\#} : X.\PshMod \to X.\Modules\) is Mathlib's sheafification functor
136	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) specialised to the identity
137	  morphism of the underlying presheaf of rings of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (a locally
138	  bijective map). Applying it to the objectwise tensor presheaf is what defines the
139	  sheaf tensor product \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}.
140	\end{definition}
141	
142	\begin{definition}[Unit module of a scheme]
143	  \label{def:unitModule}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.unitModule}
145	  \uses{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
146	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{unit module} \(\mathbf{1}_X \in X.\Modules\) is the
147	  structure sheaf viewed as a module over itself, i.e.\ Mathlib's
148	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules.unit}\) (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) for
149	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It is the value of the zeroth tensor power,
150	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}).
151	\end{definition}
152	
153	\begin{definition}\leanok
154	[Tensor product of sheaves of modules]
155	  \label{def:sheafTensorObj}
156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
157	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
158	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
159	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Tensor product" (Tag
160	  % 01CA), prose definition (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
161	  % L2282-L2297).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
163	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space and let
164	  %  $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
165	  %  We define first the {\it tensor product presheaf}
166	  %  $$
167	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
168	  %  $$
169	  %  as the rule which assigns to $U \subset X$ open the
170	  %  $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module
171	  %  $\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)$.
172	  %  Having defined this we define the {\it tensor product sheaf}
173	  %  as the sheafification of the above:
174	  %  $$
175	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
176	  %  =
177	  %  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p, \mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^\#
178	  %  $$"
179	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CA (tensor
180	  product).}
181	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Their
182	  \emph{tensor product} is the sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
183	  \[
184	    \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
185	      \;=\; \bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\bigr)^{\#},
186	  \]
187	  the sheafification (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}) of the
188	  tensor product presheaf \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\),
189	  whose value on an open \(U\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module
190	  \(\mathcal{F}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{G}(U)\). Concretely, the
191	  tensor product presheaf is the Mathlib presheaf-level monoidal product
192	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) of the underlying presheaves of
193	  modules of \(\mathcal{F}\) and \(\mathcal{G}\), and
194	  \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\) is its sheafification. This
195	  construction is symmetric and associative because the presheaf tensor product
196	  is, and sheafification preserves the coherence isomorphisms.
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}\leanok
200	[Tensor power of a line bundle]
201	  \label{def:sheafTensorPow}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow}
203	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
204	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
205	  % `definition-powers' (Tag 01CU) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex,
206	  % L4220-L4228).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Given an invertible sheaf
209	  %  $\mathcal{L}$ on $X$ and $n \in \mathbf{Z}$ we define the
210	  %  $n$th {\it tensor power} $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}$ of $\mathcal{L}$
211	  %  as the image of $\mathcal{O}_X$ under applying the equivalence
212	  %  $\mathcal{F} \mapsto\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}$
213	  %  exactly $n$ times."
214	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (tensor
215	  powers).}
216	  Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
217	  an invertible sheaf / line bundle \(L_s\)). For \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the
218	  \emph{\(m\)th tensor power} \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is defined by recursion
219	  on \(m\):
220	  \[
221	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X
222	      \quad(\text{the unit module, \cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}}),
223	    \qquad
224	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)}
225	      = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}
226	        \quad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}).
227	  \]
228	  We restrict to non-negative powers, which is all the section graded ring uses;
229	  the Stacks definition extends \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n}\) to all
230	  \(n \in \mathbf{Z}\) using invertibility, but negative powers are not needed
231	  here. For \(\mathcal{L}\) invertible each \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}\) is again
232	  invertible.
233	\end{definition}
234	
235	\begin{lemma}[Zeroth tensor power is the unit]
236	  \label{lem:tensorPow_zero}
237	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_zero}
238	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:unitModule}
239	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
240	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathbf{1}_X\), the unit module
241	  (\cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of the recursive definition
242	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
243	\end{lemma}
244	
245	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power recursion step]
246	  \label{lem:tensorPow_succ}
247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPow_succ}
248	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
249	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) and \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) one has
250	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
251	  \mathcal{L}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); this is the successor clause of
252	  \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}, holding by definition.
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{definition}\leanok
256	[Twist of a coherent sheaf by a tensor power]
257	  \label{def:sheafModuleTwist}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow}
259	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:sheafTensorPow}
260	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (in the application
261	  a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\)) and \(\mathcal{L}\) a line bundle. For
262	  \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) the \emph{\(m\)-twist} of \(\mathcal{F}\) by
263	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is
264	  \[
265	    \mathcal{F}(m) \;:=\;
266	      \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m}
267	      \qquad(\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:sheafTensorPow}),
268	  \]
269	  the degree-\(m\) carrier of the section graded module. By construction
270	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{O}_X \cong
271	  \mathcal{F}\).
272	\end{definition}
273	
274	\begin{lemma}[Zeroth twist]
275	  \label{lem:moduleTensorPow_zero}
276	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.moduleTensorPow_zero}
277	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule,
278	    def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
279	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{L}\) on \(X\) one has
280	  \(\mathcal{F}(0) = \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X\)
281	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}, \cref{def:unitModule}); this is the base clause of
282	  \cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}, holding by definition, and is canonically
283	  isomorphic to \(\mathcal{F}\) via the right unitor
284	  (\cref{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}).
285	\end{lemma}
286	
287	The next four isomorphisms are the parts of the (would-be) symmetric monoidal
288	structure on \(X.\Modules\) that descend through sheafification from
289	\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} using only \emph{functoriality} of the
290	sheafification functor (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) and, for the
291	unitors, the reflective counit isomorphism --- no strong monoidality of
292	sheafification is required. They are the structural inputs of the tensor-power
293	comparison \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
294	
295	\begin{definition}[Counit isomorphism of module sheafification]
296	  \label{def:sheafificationCounitIso}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sheafificationCounitIso}
298	  \uses{def:schemeModuleSheafification, lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
299	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) the counit of the sheafification
300	  adjunction (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
301	  \cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) gives a canonical isomorphism
302	  \((\mathcal{G})^{\#} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) of sheaves of modules,
303	  invertible because the right adjoint forgetful functor to presheaves of modules
304	  is fully faithful.
305	\end{definition}
306	
307	\begin{definition}[Left unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
308	  \label{def:tensorObjUnitIso}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjUnitIso}
310	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
311	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
312	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical left-unitor
313	  isomorphism \(\mathbf{1}_X \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
314	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
315	  \cref{def:unitModule}), obtained by sheafifying the presheaf left unitor of
316	  \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} and composing with the counit
317	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}. It is the base case
318	  (\(m = 0\)) of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
319	\end{definition}
320	
321	\begin{definition}[Right unitor of the sheaf tensor product]
322	  \label{def:tensorObjRightUnitor}
323	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjRightUnitor}
324	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:unitModule, def:sheafificationCounitIso,
325	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
326	  For a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical right-unitor
327	  isomorphism \(\mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathbf{1}_X
328	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G}\) (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj},
329	  \cref{def:unitModule}), the sheafification of the presheaf right unitor
330	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) composed with the counit
331	  isomorphism \cref{def:sheafificationCounitIso}.
332	\end{definition}
333	
334	\begin{definition}[Braiding of the sheaf tensor product]
335	  \label{def:tensorBraiding}
336	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorBraiding}
337	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, def:schemeModuleSheafification,
338	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
339	  For sheaves of modules \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) on \(X\) there is a canonical
340	  braiding isomorphism \(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}
341	  \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{G} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}\)
342	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), obtained by sheafifying
343	  (\cref{def:schemeModuleSheafification}) the symmetric braiding of the presheaf
344	  monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). It is the
345	  symmetry used in the inductive step of \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}.
346	\end{definition}
347	
348	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
349	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
351	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
352	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
353	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
354	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
355	  the sheafification adjunction
356	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
357	  presheaves of modules, and write
358	  \[
359	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
360	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
361	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
362	  \]
363	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
364	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
365	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
366	  \[
367	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
368	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
369	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
370	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
371	  \]
372	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
373	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
374	\end{lemma}
375	
376	\begin{proof}
377	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
378	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
379	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
380	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
381	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
382	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
383	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
384	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
385	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
386	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
387	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
388	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
389	
390	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
391	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
392	  is the objectwise relative tensor
393	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
394	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
395	  maps
396	  \[
397	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
398	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
399	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
400	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
401	  \]
402	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
403	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
404	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
405	  pair
406	  \[
407	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
408	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
409	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
410	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
411	  \]
412	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
413	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
414	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
415	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
416	
417	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
418	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
419	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
420	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
421	  of \(a\)-images. Now the localization unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}}\) lies in
422	  \(J.W\), and the class \(J.W\) is \emph{monoidal} for the
423	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor: whiskering a local isomorphism by
424	  \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) again yields a local isomorphism. (This is the
425	  abelian monoidality of \(J.W\), itself proved through the fact that the
426	  internal hom into a sheaf is again a sheaf.) Consequently the two whiskered
427	  comparison maps
428	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) and
429	  \(a\bigl(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
430	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\bigr)\) relating the two rows are
431	  isomorphisms.
432	
433	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
434	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
435	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
436	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
437	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
438	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
439	\end{proof}
440	
441	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
442	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
444	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
445	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
446	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
447	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
448	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
449	  \[
450	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
451	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
452	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
453	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
454	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
455	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
456	  \]
457	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
458	  arguments.
459	\end{corollary}
460	
461	\begin{proof}
462	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
463	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
464	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
465	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
466	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
467	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
468	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
469	  \[
470	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
471	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
472	    \qquad
473	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
474	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
475	  \]
476	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
477	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
478	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
479	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
480	  \[
481	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
482	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
483	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
484	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
485	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
486	  \]
487	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
488	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
489	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
490	  morphism
491	  \[
492	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
493	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
494	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
495	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
496	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
497	  \]
498	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
499	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
500	  \[
501	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
502	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
503	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
504	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
505	  \]
506	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
507	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
508	  composite
509	  \[
510	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}}
511	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
512	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
513	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},
514	  \]
515	  an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
516	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,
517	  of the whiskerings, and of the presheaf associator.
518	\end{proof}
519	
520	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
521	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
523	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
525	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
526	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
527	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
528	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
529	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
530	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
531	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
532	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
533	  %  (formulation omitted)."
534	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
535	  power isomorphisms).}
536	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
537	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
538	  \[
539	    \mu_{m,m'} :
540	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
541	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
542	  \]
543	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
544	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
545	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
546	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
547	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
548	\end{lemma}
549	
550	\begin{proof}
551	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
552	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
553	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
554	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
555	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
556	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
557	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
558	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
559	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
560	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
561	
562	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
563	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
564	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
565	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
566	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
567	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
568	  sheaf tensor products
569	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
570	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
571	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
572	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
573	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
574	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
575	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
576	  of the tensor product is never used.
577	
578	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
579	  \(m = 0\),
580	  \[
581	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
582	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
583	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
584	  \]
585	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
586	  recursive definition
587	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
588	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
589	  \[
590	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
591	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
592	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
593	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
594	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
595	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
596	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
597	  \]
598	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
599	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
600	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
601	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
602	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
603	  \[
604	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
605	      \mathcal{L}
606	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
607	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
608	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
609	  \]
610	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
611	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
612	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
613	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
614	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
615	
616	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
617	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
618	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
619	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
620	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
621	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
622	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
623	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
624	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
625	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
626	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
627	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
628	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
629	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
630	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
631	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
632	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
633	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
634	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
635	\end{proof}
636	
637	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
638	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
639	
640	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
641	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
642	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
643	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
644	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
645	global sections.
646	
647	\begin{definition}\leanok
648	[Section multiplication]
649	  \label{def:sectionMul}
650	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
651	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
652	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
653	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
654	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
655	  \[
656	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
657	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
658	    \;\longrightarrow\;
659	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
660	  \]
661	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
662	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
663	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
664	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
665	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
666	  the sheafification unit
667	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
668	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
669	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
670	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
671	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
672	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
673	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
674	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
675	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
676	\end{definition}
677	
678	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
679	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
680	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
681	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
682	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital
683	  in the following sense. For sheaves \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}, \mathcal{H}\)
684	  the two composites
685	  \[
686	    \Gamma(\mathcal{F}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{G}) \otimes \Gamma(\mathcal{H})
687	    \longrightarrow
688	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) \otimes \mathcal{H}\bigr)
689	    \cong
690	    \Gamma\bigl(\mathcal{F} \otimes (\mathcal{G} \otimes \mathcal{H})\bigr)
691	  \]
692	  agree after transport along the associator of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}; and
693	  the unit element \(1 \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
694	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}) acts as a two-sided identity, i.e.\ the section
695	  multiplication by \(1\) is, after the unitor isomorphism
696	  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\), the identity on
697	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\).
698	\end{lemma}
699	
700	\begin{proof}
701	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
702	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
703	  Both statements reduce to the corresponding identities at the level of the
704	  tensor product presheaf, where by \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}
705	  evaluation at the top open is strictly monoidal, so the associator and unitors
706	  act on elementary tensors by the usual formulas
707	  \((\sigma \otimes \tau) \otimes \upsilon \mapsto
708	  \sigma \otimes (\tau \otimes \upsilon)\) and
709	  \(1 \otimes \sigma \mapsto \sigma\). The sheafification unit \(\eta\) is a
710	  natural transformation, so it commutes with the associator and unitor
711	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}); applying \(\Gamma(X,-)\)
712	  to the naturality squares transports these presheaf-level identities to the
713	  section multiplications.
714	\end{proof}
715	
716	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
717	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
718	
719	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
720	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
721	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
722	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
723	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
724	
725	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
726	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
727	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
728	  \mathlibok
729	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
730	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
731	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
732	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
733	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
734	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
735	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
736	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
737	  \(1 \in A_0\).
738	\end{lemma}
739	
740	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
741	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
742	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
743	  \mathlibok
744	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
745	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
746	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
747	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
748	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
749	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
750	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
751	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
752	  extension of the graded action.
753	\end{lemma}
754	
755	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
756	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
757	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
758	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
759	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
760	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
762	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
763	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
765	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
766	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
767	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
768	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
769	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
770	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
771	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
772	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
773	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
774	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
775	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
776	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
777	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
778	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
779	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
780	  multiplication is the composite
781	  \[
782	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
783	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
784	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
785	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
786	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
787	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
788	  \]
789	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
790	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
791	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
792	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
793	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
794	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
795	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
796	\end{lemma}
797	
798	\begin{proof}
799	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
800	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
801	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
802	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
803	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
804	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
805	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
806	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
807	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
808	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
809	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
810	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
811	  commutative-semiring structure.
812	\end{proof}

814	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
815	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
816	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
817	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
818	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
819	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
820	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
821	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
822	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
823	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
824	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
825	  %  $$
826	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
827	  %  =
828	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
829	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
830	  %  $$
831	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
832	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
833	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
834	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
835	  \(X_s\). The family
836	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
837	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
838	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
839	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
840	  action
841	  \[
842	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
843	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
844	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
845	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
846	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
847	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
848	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
849	  \]
850	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
851	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
852	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
853	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
854	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
855	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
856	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
857	\end{lemma}
858	
859	\begin{proof}
860	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
861	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
862	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---
863	  compatibility of the action with the ring multiplication
864	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
865	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of
866	  the section multiplication (\cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent}) together with the
867	  associativity and commutativity constraints of the comparison isomorphisms
868	  (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), exactly as in the proof of
869	  \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring} but with the third tensor factor
870	  carrying \(\mathcal{F}_s\). These are the axioms of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), so
871	  \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib} produces the module structure of
872	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over \(R(X_s, L_s)\).
873	\end{proof}

1627	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}

1641	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) out of the associated sheaf of \(N\) (the case

1643	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\)). Then \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite

1649	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}

1652	  lemma's two source-side hypotheses for \(\mathcal{G} = \widetilde{N}\). By the affine
1653	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma\) adjunction \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the unit

1665	\begin{lemma}[G1 base case — finite affine sections from a finite generating family]
1666	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1667	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1668	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1669	        lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1670	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules of
1671	  finite type, and \(D \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose the restriction
1672	  \(\F|_D = (\mathrm{pullback}\,D.\iota)^{*}\F\) carries a finite generating family
1673	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1674	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1675	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1676	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement.
1677	\end{lemma}
1678	
1679	\begin{proof}
1680	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1681	        lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1682	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1683	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1684	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1685	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1686	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1687	
1688	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1689	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1690	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1691	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1692	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1693	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1694	
1695	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1696	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1697	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1698	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1699	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1700	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1701	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1702	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1703	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1704	
1705	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1706	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1707	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1708	
1709	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1710	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1711	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1712	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1713	\end{proof}

1757	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1758	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1759	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1760	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1761	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1762	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1763	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1764	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1765	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1766	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1767	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1768	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1769	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1770	\end{lemma}
1771	
1772	\begin{proof}
1773	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1774	  Throughout, flatness is established by the standard locality criteria for module flatness
1775	  over a commutative ring, applied on the \emph{base} ring; the source cover \(\{W_j\}\)
1776	  enters only through the stalks of \(\F\), and is never treated as a covering family of any
1777	  base ring.
1778	
1779	  \emph{Step 1 — free \(\Rightarrow\) flat on each patch.} Each \((M_j)_f\) is free over
1780	  \(A_f\) by hypothesis, and a free module is flat. Hence each \((M_j)_f\) is flat over
1781	  \(A_f\).
1782	
1783	  \emph{Step 2 — pointwise flatness of \(\F\) over the base on \(p^{-1}(V)\).} Fix a point
1784	  \(x \in p^{-1}(V) = X_V\) and put \(s = p(x) \in V = D(f)\). The cover \(\{W_j\}\) of
1785	  \(p^{-1}(U_0) \supseteq p^{-1}(V)\) contains a patch \(W_j \ni x\). On \(W_j\),
1786	  quasi-coherence identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of
1787	  the finite \(B_j\)-module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\) (finite by
1788	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}); consequently the sections of \(\F\) over a
1789	  basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W_j\) are the localization \((M_j)_g\)
1790	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}), and passing to the limit the stalk
1791	  \(\F_x\) is the localization of \(M_j\) at the prime of \(B_j\) corresponding to \(x\).
1792	  Because \(s \in D(f)\), the element \(f\) is invertible in the local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) and
1793	  hence acts invertibly on \(\F_x\); thus \(\F_x\) is equally a localization of the inverted
1794	  module \((M_j)_f\). The local ring \(\OO_{S,s}\) is the localization of \(A_f\) at the prime
1795	  of \(s\) (a point of \(D(f) = \Spec A_f\)), and localization preserves flatness; since
1796	  \((M_j)_f\) is flat over \(A_f\), the localized module \(\F_x\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,s}\).
1797	  Therefore \(\F\) is flat over the base at every point of \(p^{-1}(V)\): the stalk \(\F_x\)
1798	  is a flat \(\OO_{S,p(x)}\)-module for all \(x \in p^{-1}(V)\).
1799	
1800	  \emph{Step 3 — from pointwise flatness to \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).}
1801	  Let \(U \le V\) and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) be affine, and write \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\),
1802	  \(B = \Gamma(X, W)\), and \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\); here \(N\) is a \(B\)-module and \(B\) an
1803	  \(R\)-algebra. We prove \(N\) flat over \(R\) by reducing first on the base, then on the
1804	  source.
1805	
1806	  \emph{Base reduction.} By the base-maximal locality criterion for flatness it suffices to
1807	  show, for every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\), that the localization
1808	  \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R\); equivalently, that \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over
1809	  the local ring \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\), where \(x \in U \subseteq V = D(f)\) is the
1810	  point corresponding to \(\mathfrak p\). In particular \(\OO_{S,x}\) is a localization of
1811	  \(A_f\).
1812	
1813	  \emph{Source reduction.} Flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over the local base
1814	  \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\) is checked at the points of the source: it suffices that
1815	  for every point \(y \in W\) lying over a generization of \(x\) the stalk \(\F_y\) — the
1816	  localization of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) at the prime of \(B \otimes_R R_{\mathfrak p}\)
1817	  corresponding to \(y\) — is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p} = \OO_{S,x}\). Each such \(y\) lies
1818	  in \(p^{-1}(V)\), so by Step 2 \(\F_y\) is flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\). As \(p(y)\) is a
1819	  generization of \(x\), the comparison \(\OO_{S,x} \to \OO_{S,p(y)}\) is a localization,
1820	  hence flat, and transitivity of flatness upgrades ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,p(y)}\)''
1821	  to ``\(\F_y\) flat over \(\OO_{S,x}\)''. The passage from these stalk-flatness statements
1822	  to flatness of \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) records that module flatness
1823	  over a local base ring is detected at the points of the source affine.
1824	
1825	  Combining the two reductions, \(N_{\mathfrak p}\) is flat over \(R_{\mathfrak p}\) for
1826	  every maximal ideal \(\mathfrak p \subset R\); hence \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over
1827	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1828	\end{proof}
1829	
1830	
1831	\begin{theorem}
1832	
1833	
1834	
1835	\leanok
1836	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1837	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1838	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1839	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1840	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1841	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1842	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1843	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1844	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1845	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1846	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1847	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1848	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1849	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1850	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1851	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1852	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1853	
1854	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1855	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1856	\end{theorem}
1857	
1858	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1859	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1860	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1861	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1862	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1863	% by restricting to a non-empty
1864	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1865	\begin{proof}
1866	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1867	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1868	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1869	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1870	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1871	
1872	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1873	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1874	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1875	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1876	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1877	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1878	
1879	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1880	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1881	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1882	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1883	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1884	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1885	
1886	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1887	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1888	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1889	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1890	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1891	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1892	
1893	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1894	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1895	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1896	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1897	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1898	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1899	\end{proof}

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43

52	% reference).

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
